1:
On Luke 4:1–2 — “And Jesus being full of the Holy Ghost returned from Jordan, and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness, being forty days tempted of the devil” — the witnesses open the temptation and weigh its purpose.

The Eastern fathers read the timing and the manhood. Theophylact: Christ is tempted “after His baptism, showing us that after we are baptized, temptations await us.” Basil marks that, since “not to suffer hunger is above the nature of man, our Lord took upon Himself the feeling of hunger… not forced… but as if provoking the devil to the conflict.” The Latin Ambrose: “He was led… into the wilderness… that He might provoke the devil; for if the one had not contended, the other… had not conquered” — and mystically, to recall the exiled Adam from the wilderness to which he was cast out of Paradise.

The grammatical-historical commentators harmonise the Synoptists. Robertson notes the allusion to the descent of the Spirit at the baptism (Luke 3:21), and that Matthew’s “led” and Mark’s stronger “driveth forth” agree in substance. Barnes adds that during the forty days “he was sustained by the power of God,” the recorded temptations falling at their close (cf. Exodus 34:28; 1 Kings 19:8). The Puritan Henry sees the Second Adam, “the seed of the woman,” entering the lists to baffle the serpent who had foiled the first.

So the witnesses set the temptation in its place: full of the Spirit and fresh from his anointing, the Lord goes not to ease but to conflict, fasting like Moses and Elijah their forty days, that he might meet the tempter on our behalf and overcome where Adam fell. Lightfoot stands apart with a literalist conjecture — that the Spirit “caught up” Christ bodily into the wilderness, as Philip was caught away (Acts 8:39) — but the common sense of the witnesses is that the Spirit led him, by an inward impulse, into the appointed field of trial.

So the witnesses set the scene of the great conflict. The Eastern Cyril marks that, since we are now “made partakers of the Divine nature” by the regeneration of water and the Spirit, the Spirit who rested on Christ rests also on the baptized — and so, Theophylact adds, temptation follows our baptism as it followed his. Henry presses the figure: here is the Second Adam, “the seed of the woman,” entering the lists to baffle the serpent “who by one temptation had baffled… our first parents,” making reprisals upon him “in the beginning of the war” and conquering the conqueror. The verse thus opens the temptation not as a private trial but as the second round of the ancient war — the Spirit-filled Christ going down, on our behalf, to meet the enemy who had ruined Adam, and to win where the first man lost.
2:
On Luke 4:2 — “being forty days tempted of the devil… and in those days he did eat nothing: and when they were ended, he afterward hungered” — the witnesses dwell on the fast and the reality of the hunger.

The grammatical-historical commentators clarify the order. Barnes: “through forty days he was tried in various ways,” but the three recorded temptations “did not take place until the forty days were finished” (Matthew 4:2); through it all “he was sustained by the power of God during this season of extraordinary fasting.” Robertson reads the present passive participle “being tempted” as parallel to “was led,” the contest running between “Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit,” and the slanderer. Lightfoot notes that Moses fasted forty days three times over, but for Christ, “having withal so great a conflict with the devil,” it sufficed to do it once.

The verse joins the supernatural to the genuinely human. For forty days the Lord ate nothing, sustained by God; and yet, when they were ended, he “afterward hungered” — the true want of a true body, which the tempter would seize as his opening. The fast was no mere display but the appointed arena, recalling Israel’s forty years of trial in the same wilderness, and Moses and Elijah’s forty days with God.

So the witnesses guard both natures at the threshold of the trial. The hunger was real, that the temptation might be real; the sustaining was divine, that the forty days might be possible. The Lord who would be tempted “in all points like as we are” first took our weakness upon him, entering the contest not as one shielded from need but as one who knew it — and so meeting the adversary, on the ground of a felt human hunger, that he might overcome for us where our first father, amid the plenty of Eden, had been overcome.

So the witnesses weigh the forty days. Lightfoot recalls that Moses fasted forty days three times over in his dealings with God; for Christ, “having withal so great a conflict with the devil,” it sufficed to fast but once. The number itself echoes Israel’s forty years of trial in this same wilderness, and Moses’ and Elijah’s forty days with God — so that the Lord, fasting, gathers up the whole history of God’s tried people into himself. And the hunger that closes the fast is the door by which the tempter enters: the body’s real and lawful want is to be made the occasion of an unlawful suggestion. The witnesses thus frame the trial as both supernatural and human — a fast no man could keep unaided, ending in a hunger any man would feel, that the Son might be tempted in our nature and for our sake.
3:
On Luke 4:3 — “And the devil said unto him, If thou be the Son of God, command this stone that it be made bread” — the witnesses read the first assault, aimed at the appetite.

The Eastern Cyril of Alexandria sets the scene grandly: the prophets spoke of the Only-begotten Word, “equal unto God in glory,” who yet “continued in the Spirit in the wilderness forty days… and when they were accomplished He hungered” — the eternal Word stooping to be tried. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks that “the Son of God” here is anarthrous, pointing to the relationship rather than the office, a “manifest reference to the words of the Father” at the baptism (Luke 3:22); the devil’s “if” is a condition of the first class — he assumes the Sonship and turns it to temptation. Ellicott notes Luke’s vivid singular “this stone,” perhaps a particular round loaf-shaped stone, as against Matthew’s “these stones.”

The subtlety of the first temptation lies just here. It does not deny the Sonship but presumes upon it: let the Son use his power to serve his own hunger, independently of the Father’s will. To turn the stone to bread would be no sin in itself; the sin lay in the suggestion that the Son should provide for himself outside the path of trust and obedience in which the Father had set him.

So the witnesses see the tempter probe the appetite first, as he had done with Adam. The Lord, truly hungry, is invited to step out of the place of dependence and command relief by his own power. The answer, drawn from Deuteronomy, will set obedience to God’s word above the body’s craving. Here the witnesses mark the pattern: the devil grants the title “Son of God” only to misuse it, urging the Son to a self-willed use of his power that would break the very trust in the Father which his hunger was meant to prove.

So the witnesses read the first dart, aimed at the appetite as it was at Adam’s. Robertson notes the devil “assumes that Jesus is Son of God,” and even the “this stone” may point to a particular loaf-shaped stone before them. The subtlety is that the deed urged is not sinful in itself — the Son could turn stone to bread — but the doing of it, at the tempter’s prompting and to escape the Father’s appointed want, would break the trust in which the Son was set. Cyril’s wonder frames it: the eternal Word, “equal unto God in glory,” has stooped to hunger, and is now tempted to use his Godhead to serve himself. The witnesses see here the perennial snare — to make even our powers and rights the means of stepping out of dependence on God, and to relieve a real need by a self-willed path he has not opened.
4:
On Luke 4:4 — “And Jesus answered him, saying, It is written, That man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God” — the witnesses note the Lord’s first weapon, the written word.

The voices here are brief, the commentators chiefly marking the source of the quotation. Barnes and Wesley point to its origin in Deuteronomy 8:3 — the lesson Israel learned in the same wilderness, fed with manna, “that he might make thee know that man doth not live by bread only.” The Reformed Gill marks simply that Jesus answered “It is written,” meeting the tempter not with a display of power but with Scripture.

The reply is itself a refusal of the temptation’s premise. The devil had urged the Son to secure life by bread of his own making; the Lord answers that life rests not on bread alone but on “every word of God” — on the Father’s providing will, in whose hand are all the means of life. To trust that word, even in hunger, is to live; to grasp at bread outside it, even at the prompting of real need, is to forsake the source of life for its mere instrument.

So the witnesses find in this first answer the model of all victory over temptation. The Lord does not argue with the tempter, nor reason from his own dignity, but takes “the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God,” and quotes the very Scripture given to Israel in their wilderness trial. Where the first Adam fell by doubting and disobeying God’s word, the Second Adam stands by trusting and obeying it — teaching his people that the surest defence in the hour of want is not the assertion of our rights or powers, but a humble submission to the word and will of God.

So the witnesses find in the first answer the model of victory. The Lord does not dispute, nor display his power, but takes “the sword of the Spirit” and quotes the lesson God taught Israel with the manna (Deuteronomy 8:3): “man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God.” Life rests not on the bread but on the God who gives or withholds it; to trust his word in hunger is to live, to grasp at bread outside it is to forsake the spring for the stream. Where the first Adam fell by doubting and disobeying God’s word amid plenty, the Second Adam stands by trusting and obeying it amid want — teaching his people that the surest defence in need is not the assertion of our rights but humble submission to the word and will of God.
5:
On Luke 4:5 — “And the devil, taking him up into an high mountain, shewed unto him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time” — the witnesses note the second assault and the difference of order from Matthew.

The Eastern Theophylact marks the progression: “the enemy had first assailed Christ by the temptation of the appetite, as also he did Adam. He next tempts Him with the desire of gain or covetousness, showing Him all the kingdoms.” The Latin Ambrose distinguishes power from its lust: “all power is from God… the lust of power is from the evil one; power is not itself evil, but he who evilly uses it.” The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott fasten on Luke’s vivid “in a moment of time” (stigmē, a point, found only here) — a mental panorama flashed before the mind, “a great feat” of the tempter, eminently characteristic of the soul’s power to compass vast images in an instant.

On the order, Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott observe that Luke makes this the second temptation where Matthew makes it the third (and last). Gill judges Matthew’s order “the true and genuine one,” Luke relating the facts “without attending to the strict order”; yet Luke does not contradict Matthew, for he does not say “then” as Matthew does.

So the witnesses read the second snare as the lure of dominion: having failed at the appetite, the tempter offers the kingdoms of the world, the bait of covetous ambition. The vision was no real survey of empires but an instantaneous picture set before the mind, the glory of all earthly rule held out in a breath. And the witnesses draw the moral: power and honour are God’s gifts, good when bestowed and rightly used, but the craving to seize them, and to gain them by the devil’s terms, is itself the temptation — the very ambition by which the tempter himself had fallen.

So the witnesses read the second snare, the lure of dominion. Lightfoot drily measures the “moment of time” the rabbis reckoned — the fifty-eight-thousandth part of an hour — but the point is that the whole glory of the kingdoms was flashed before the mind in an instant. Ambrose draws the moral: power is from God and not evil in itself, “good if it is bestowed… not if it is seized”; the sin is in the lust to grasp it, and on the tempter’s terms. So the witnesses mark how the assault rises from the body to the soul’s wider ambition: having failed at hunger, the devil offers empire. And they note the difference of order from Matthew without contradiction — Luke relating the facts, Matthew the sequence — both agreeing that the bait of the world’s glory was set before the Son who would win the world another way.
6:
On Luke 4:6 — “All this power will I give thee, and the glory of them: for that is delivered unto me; and to whomsoever I will I give it” — the witnesses weigh the tempter’s vast and lying claim.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that Luke’s report is the more specific. Robertson: “Satan here claims possession of world power and Jesus does not deny it” — a usurped dominion, due to man’s sin and by God’s permission, but the boast “to whomsoever I will I give it” is the devil’s vaunt. Ellicott marks that the specific assertion of a delegated sovereignty is “peculiar to St. Luke,” and that the notion of any such grant to the Tempter “has… no foundation in Scripture.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley flatly denies the claim: “Not so, Satan. It is God, not thou, that putteth down one, and setteth up another,” though Satan, “by God’s permission, may occasion great revolutions in the world.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem agrees that “riches, and gold, and silver are not… the devil’s”: God says by the prophet, “The gold is Mine… and to whomsoever I will I give it.”

So the witnesses expose the half-truth on which the temptation rests. The devil has a real but borrowed and limited sway over a fallen world — he is “the prince of this world” — and there is enough truth in his power to make the lie plausible. But his sweeping “to whomsoever I will I give it” usurps the prerogative of God, who alone raises up and casts down. To accept the offer would be to receive from the tempter what is the Father’s to give, and to hold the kingdoms on Satan’s terms rather than to inherit them, as the Son will, through obedience and the cross.

So the witnesses expose the half-truth on which the offer rests. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem grants that the devil has a real but borrowed sway — yet “the gold is Mine, and the silver is Mine,” says God; “do thou but use it well, and there is no fault to be found with money.” Ellicott insists the notion of any sovereignty “delegated” to the Tempter “has no foundation in Scripture.” The devil’s “to whomsoever I will I give it” usurps the very prerogative of God, who alone raises up and casts down. So the witnesses see the danger of the plausible lie: there is enough truth in Satan’s power over a fallen world to make the boast credible, but to take the kingdoms from his hand would be to hold them on his terms — to receive as the devil’s gift what is the Father’s to bestow, and to win the world by forfeiting God.
7:
On Luke 4:7 — “If thou therefore wilt worship me, all shall be thine” — the witnesses mark the naked blasphemy at the heart of the offer.

The Reformed Gill draws the contrast between the angels: “a good angel will not suffer himself to be worshipped… but directs to the worship of God only,” while “a fallen angel… seeks to be worshipped… even by the Son of God himself, whom all the holy angels worship.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the delicacy of Luke’s “worship before me” (where Matthew has the blunter “worship me”), an ingressive aorist — “just bow the knee once up here in my presence” — the condition put in a “very emphatic and subtle way.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke refers the fuller treatment to the notes on Matthew 4:1.

Here the tempter at last unmasks himself. The whole world had been dangled as the bait; the price is a single act of homage. “If thou wilt worship me” — the creature, and a fallen one, demands of the Creator the worship due to God alone. Whatever subtlety had clothed the earlier assaults, this is open rebellion: Satan asks to be put in the place of God.

So the witnesses see the temptation reach its true point. To gain the kingdoms by worshipping the devil would be to win the world by losing God — to purchase a borrowed and passing dominion at the cost of the one allegiance that is the soul’s life. The very baseness of the demand, when stripped of its glittering wrapping, exposes the lie of all such bargains: that the things offered are not the tempter’s to give, that the homage he asks is treason against God, and that the short road to glory which avoids the Father’s will leads not to a throne but to the worship of the evil one.

So the witnesses lay bare the blasphemy beneath the bargain. Gill sets the fallen angel against the holy: a good angel refuses worship and points men to God, but Satan “seeks to be worshipped… even by the Son of God himself, whom all the holy angels worship.” Robertson notes the subtle, ingressive form — “just bow the knee once… in my presence.” The whole world had been the bait; the price is a single act of homage to the creature in the place of God. So the witnesses see the temptation reach its true point and its true ugliness: to gain the kingdoms by worshipping the devil would be to win the world by losing God, to purchase a borrowed and passing dominion at the cost of the soul’s one allegiance. The glittering offer, unwrapped, is naked treason against the Most High.
8:
On Luke 4:8 — “Get thee behind me, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve” — the witnesses hear the decisive answer.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that “Satan used this verb… to Jesus who turns it against him” with the quotation from Deuteronomy 6:13: Jesus “clearly perceived that one could not worship both Satan and God. He had to choose whom he would serve.” Barnes points to the same Scripture (Deuteronomy 6:13; Deuteronomy 10:20). The Reformed Gill marks that Jesus spoke the words “with indignation… and detestation of his proposals.”

The answer cuts to the root. Against the demand for worship the Lord sets the first commandment: worship and service belong to God alone. The very word the tempter had used — “worship” — the Lord turns back upon him, declaring by Scripture that to render it to any but God is forbidden. There is no negotiation, only the sword of the word and the command to depart.

So the witnesses find in this third answer, as in the first two, the pattern of victory: “it is written.” The Lord meets the climactic temptation not with his own authority but with the plain commandment of God, refusing in a single stroke both the homage demanded and the kingdoms offered. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem sets this sinless steadfastness in its larger frame — that Christ, “being then in the flesh like others, was crucified, but not for the like sins,” never “led to death for covetousness,” for he was “a Teacher of poverty.” The Son who would not worship the devil to gain the world will instead win it by obedience unto death.

So the witnesses find, in the third answer as in the first two, the sword of “it is written.” The Lord turns the devil’s own word “worship” against him with Deuteronomy 6:13: worship and service belong to God alone, and “one could not worship both Satan and God.” There is no parley, only the command and the Scripture. Cyril of Jerusalem sets the steadfastness in its frame: Christ, “a Teacher of poverty,” was never “led to death for covetousness.” So the witnesses mark that the Son who refused to worship the devil for the world will instead win the world by obedience unto death — the short road of homage to Satan declined for the long road of the cross. The verse teaches that every bargain which would gain us the world at the price of our allegiance to God is to be met not with negotiation but with the plain commandment, and the tempter bidden depart.
9:
On Luke 4:9 — the tempter sets Jesus on the pinnacle of the temple, saying, “If thou be the Son of God, cast thyself down” — the witnesses read the last assault, the temptation of presumption.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read it as the snare of vainglory. Ambrose: this is the weapon “of boasting, which always causes the offender to fall down” — “while a man thinks he is climbing higher, he is by his pretension to lofty deeds brought low.” Origen pictures the Lord as a willing wrestler, going gladly to meet the trial, “Lead me where you will, and you will find me the stronger in every thing.” Athanasius notes the tempter “came to Him as man, but not finding in Him the marks of his ancient seed” — no taint of sin — “he departed.”

The Latin Augustine addresses the order: “Matthew relates… in a similar manner, but not in the same order,” and it is “uncertain… which took place first” — yet “it matters little… as long as it is clear that they all took place.” The Eastern Maximus reads a further mystery: the devil, foiled in the wilderness, would afterward stir up the Gentiles and Pharisees against him.

So the witnesses see the final temptation invert the first two. Having failed to make the Son distrust the Father (turn stones to bread) and failed to make him forsake the Father (worship me), the tempter now urges a false trust: presume upon the Father’s care, cast thyself down, and force a rescue. It is the subtlest snare, baited even with Scripture. And the fathers find the Lord meeting it as the unconquerable wrestler in whom Satan finds nothing of his own — the sinless Son who will neither doubt, nor desert, nor presume upon his Father, but keep the path of simple obedience to the end.

So the witnesses read the last assault as the snare of presumption, inverting the first two. Having failed to make the Son distrust the Father (turn stones to bread) and to make him forsake the Father (worship me), the tempter now urges a false trust — presume on the Father’s care; cast thyself down. Ambrose marks it the weapon of boasting, which “causes the offender to fall down”; Origen sees the willing wrestler, “stronger in every thing”; Athanasius, the enemy finding in Christ “not the marks of his ancient seed” — no taint of sin — and departing. Augustine leaves the order open: it matters little which came first, so all took place. The witnesses thus behold the subtlest temptation, baited even with Scripture, met by the sinless Son in whom Satan finds nothing of his own — one who will neither doubt, nor desert, nor presume, but keep the path of simple obedience to the end.
10:
On Luke 4:10 — “For it is written, He shall give his angels charge over thee, to keep thee” — the witnesses note that the tempter now wields Scripture, and how.

The voices here are brief and pointed. Barnes and Wesley identify the citation as Psalm 91:11. The Reformed Gill makes the telling observation, “long ago” noted by Jerome, that Satan, “in citing this text, has left out the middle clause” — “to keep thee in all thy ways” — the very words that would have exposed his misuse, since to fling oneself from a pinnacle is no “way” of God’s appointing.

The verse shows the tempter at his most dangerous: quoting the word of God. Foiled three times (in Matthew’s order) by “it is written,” he now meets Scripture with Scripture, citing the promise of angelic protection to coax the Son into a needless and presumptuous leap. The devil can be orthodox when it serves him; he knows the text, and bends it to his purpose.

So the witnesses draw the abiding warning. Scripture may be quoted to a false end, and the promise of God perverted into a pretext for tempting God. The mark of the tempter’s use is precisely what Gill observes — the suppressed clause, the wrenching of a word from its setting. A promise made to those who walk in God’s ways is twisted into a licence to forsake them. The defence, as the Lord will show, is not less Scripture but Scripture rightly handled: the whole counsel of God, in which one word is weighed against another, so that no promise is made the servant of presumption.

So the witnesses mark the tempter at his most dangerous — quoting the word of God. Foiled by “it is written,” he now meets Scripture with Scripture, citing the promise of angelic keeping (Psalm 91:11) to coax the Son into a needless leap. But Gill, after Jerome, exposes the fraud: Satan “has left out the middle clause” — “to keep thee in all thy ways” — the very words that would betray his misuse, since to fling oneself from a pinnacle is no “way” of God’s appointing. So the witnesses draw the lasting warning: Scripture may be quoted to a false end, a promise wrenched from its setting into a pretext for tempting God. The mark of the abuse is precisely the suppressed clause; and the defence is not less Scripture but Scripture rightly handled, the whole counsel of God weighed together, so that no promise is made the servant of presumption.
12:
On Luke 4:12 — “And Jesus answering said unto him, It is said, Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God” — the witnesses note the third and final answer from Deuteronomy.

The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the perfect passive “it is said” (eirētai), “a favourite way of quoting Scripture,” and that “each time he uses Deuteronomy against the devil” (Deuteronomy 6:16); the point is sharp — “testing God is not trusting God” (Plummer). Barnes and Wesley likewise refer the words to Deuteronomy 6:16, “ye shall not tempt the Lord your God, as ye tempted him in Massah.”

The answer unmasks the perversion of the previous verse. The tempter had quoted a promise of protection to invite a presumptuous leap; the Lord answers with a command that exposes the misuse — to demand a miraculous rescue, by needlessly courting danger, is not to trust God but to “tempt” him, to put him to the proof. The promise of Psalm 91:11 is not cancelled but rightly placed: it belongs to those who walk in God’s ways, not to those who fling themselves out of them.

So the witnesses complete the pattern of the threefold victory. Three times the tempter assaulted, and three times the Lord answered, “it is written… it is written… it is said” — each time from Deuteronomy, the book of Israel’s wilderness lessons. Where Israel had tempted God at Massah, demanding signs of his presence, the true Israel refuses to do so. The verse teaches that genuine faith does not contrive emergencies to test God’s faithfulness, but walks the appointed path and trusts him there; and that the surest answer to a misapplied Scripture is the Scripture that corrects it.

So the witnesses complete the threefold victory. The Lord answers from Deuteronomy a third time (Deuteronomy 6:16): “Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God,” the lesson of Massah, where Israel demanded signs of God’s presence. Robertson’s point is the heart of it: “testing God is not trusting God.” To court needless danger and demand a miraculous rescue is not faith but presumption, and the promise of Psalm 91:11 is not cancelled but rightly placed — it is for those who walk in God’s ways, not for those who fling themselves out of them. The witnesses thus see the true Israel refusing to do what Israel did at Massah: genuine faith does not contrive emergencies to prove God’s faithfulness, but walks the appointed path and trusts him there; and the surest answer to a misapplied text is the text that corrects it.
13:
On Luke 4:13 — “And when the devil had ended all the temptation, he departed from him for a season” — the witnesses mark the completeness of the trial and the ominous “for a season.”

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the “all.” Robertson: the three kinds “exhaust the avenues of approach (the appetites, the nerves, the ambitions)… he was in all points tempted like as we are” (Hebrews 4:15); “the enemy tried all his weapons, and was at all points defeated” (Plummer). Ellicott reads “every kind of temptation” — “the three trials… made up the cycle” then open to our Lord’s human nature. On “for a season,” Barnes and Ellicott note it implies later assaults: the devil tried him again in the agony (John 14:30) and at “the power of darkness” (Luke 22:53); Wesley names “a convenient season… in the garden of Gethsemane.”

The verse closes the wilderness contest with a double note. The temptation was complete — the tempter spent every weapon, and was wholly defeated; and yet his departure is only “for a season,” a withdrawal, not a surrender. Lightfoot notes that, having found suggestion useless against this Adversary, the devil “watches for an opportunity of trying his arts… some other way.”

So the witnesses draw the comfort and the warning together. The comfort: the Lord has met and mastered the whole cycle of temptation, in all the avenues by which it reaches us, and so is able to succour them that are tempted. The warning: a victory over temptation is not the end of temptation; the enemy retires only to return at a more “convenient season.” The same Christ who triumphed in the wilderness would be assaulted again in Gethsemane and at the cross — and his people too, having overcome in one hour, must watch, for the tempter departs only for a time.

So the witnesses close the wilderness contest on a double note. The temptation was complete — Robertson: the three “exhaust the avenues of approach (the appetites, the nerves, the ambitions)” — and wholly defeated, so that “he was in all points tempted like as we are” (Hebrews 4:15). Yet the devil departs only “for a season,” a withdrawal not a surrender; Lightfoot notes he now “watches… to try his arts some other way,” and Barnes and Wesley point on to the agony in Gethsemane and the “power of darkness” (Luke 22:53; John 14:30). The witnesses gather the comfort and the warning: the Lord has mastered the whole cycle of temptation and can succour the tempted; but a victory in one hour is not the end of temptation, for the enemy retires only to return at a more convenient season — and his people, having overcome, must still watch.
14:
On Luke 4:14 — “And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee” — the witnesses mark the victor’s return and the opening of the Galilean ministry.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the power. Origen: “the Lord having overcome the tempter, power was added to Him” — that is, in its manifestation; Bede: “by the power of the Spirit he means showing forth of miracles.” Cyril distinguishes Christ from the saints: he wrought miracles “not from any external power… as other saints, but rather as being by nature the Son of God,” his own power. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Luke here compresses, following Mark’s outline and leaving John’s “year of obscurity” unfilled; Ellicott marks “a new phase… as striking as that which passed over the Apostles at Pentecost.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the lesson of tested virtue: “He who, through the grace of God, resists and overcomes temptation, is always bettered by it” — the Lord returns “in the mighty power of the Spirit” to show forth his godhead and love. The Puritan Henry observes that, having defended himself against the devil’s assaults, Christ now “begins to act offensively” by his preaching and miracles.

So the witnesses set the return as the fruit of the victory. The same Spirit who led him into the wilderness now brings him back “in power” into Galilee, the conflict over and the public ministry begun. The fathers are careful to mark the difference between Christ and his servants: the prophets and apostles wrought wonders by a grace received; the Son wrought them by the power that was his own by nature. And the witnesses find in the sequence a word for all who are tried: temptation faithfully resisted does not weaken but strengthens, sending the believer back to his work the more filled with the Spirit, the more proved, and the more useful.

So the witnesses read the return as the fruit of the victory. The same Spirit who led him into the wilderness now brings him back “in power” into Galilee; and the fathers are careful to mark how that power differs from the saints’. Origen and Bede take it of the manifestation of miracles; Cyril presses that Christ wrought them “not from any external power… as other saints, but… as being by nature the Son of God” — his own power, not a grace received. Clarke draws the lesson for us: “he who… resists and overcomes temptation is always bettered by it.” The witnesses thus find in the sequence a word for every tried believer: temptation faithfully resisted does not weaken but strengthens, sending the servant of God back to his work the more filled with the Spirit, the more proved, and the more useful — while reserving to Christ alone the working of wonders by a power that was natively his.
15:
On Luke 4:15 — “And he taught in their synagogues, being glorified of all” — the witnesses mark the bright dawn of the ministry and its coming shadow.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the description. Robertson notes the imperfect tenses — “he was teaching” (his habit) and “being glorified” (durative) — and that the synagogues “were an open door to Jesus before the hostility of the Pharisees was aroused.” Ellicott marks “the dawn of the day of work”: “wonder, admiration, glory, waited on the new Prophet,” though it would soon turn to opposition when his preaching made its demand on faith and obedience.

The Reformed Calvin presses the divine source of the impression: Luke states this “for the express purpose of informing us, that, from the very commencement, a divine power shone in Christ, and compelled even those who cherished a malignant spirit… to join in admiring him.”

So the witnesses set this general notice before the particular scene at Nazareth. At first all is admiration: the Lord teaches freely in the synagogues, and is “glorified of all.” But the witnesses, with one eye on what follows, mark how fragile such acclaim is. Wesley: “God usually gives strong cordials after strong temptations. But neither their approbation continued long, nor the outward calm which he now enjoyed.” The early glory of the ministry is real, and traces to the divine power shining in Christ’s words; yet it is the applause of men, which the next scene will show turning swiftly to wrath when his teaching crosses their pride — a warning against resting on the changeable approval of the crowd.

So the witnesses set this general notice before the scene at Nazareth, and mark the fragility of its acclaim. Calvin presses the divine source: Luke records it to show that “from the very commencement, a divine power shone in Christ,” compelling even the malignant to admire him. Robertson reads the imperfects — the habit of teaching, the durative being-glorified — and notes the synagogues stood open “before the hostility of the Pharisees was aroused.” Yet Wesley sounds the warning: “God usually gives strong cordials after strong temptations. But neither their approbation continued long, nor the outward calm.” The witnesses thus see the bright dawn of the ministry for what it is — real, and traceable to the divine power in Christ’s words, but resting on the changeable approval of the crowd. The very next scene will show that admiration curdling into wrath when his teaching crosses men’s pride.
16:
On Luke 4:16 — “And he came to Nazareth… and, as his custom was, he went into the synagogue on the sabbath day, and stood up for to read” — the witnesses dwell on the Lord’s habit of worship and the synagogue setting.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the custom. Barnes: “the Saviour regularly attended the service of the synagogue… there was great corruption… but Christ did not on that account keep away from the place of public worship” — a weighty example. Lightfoot details the synagogue rite: the law was read standing, leaning on nothing. Ellicott notes the narrative is “peculiar to St. Luke,” likely from the same informants as his Infancy account. Robertson dates this visit before that of Mark 6:1, Jesus returning after “a year of public ministry” with a wide reputation.

The Reformed Calvin marks Christ’s method: by expounding Isaiah and “applying it to the instruction which was immediately required, he turned upon him the eyes of all” — observing, as far as he had opportunity, “the usual custom” of the synagogue. Wesley notes the posture: “the Jewish custom to read standing, but to preach sitting.”

So the witnesses set the scene of the great Nazareth sermon, and draw first its quiet lesson. The Son of God, in the town where he was reared, keeps the sabbath and goes to the synagogue “as his custom was” — honouring public worship even where doctrine and practice were corrupt, and so condemning, the commentators note, both the careless who neglect God’s house and the proud who forsake it for its faults. The Latin Tertullian adds his anti-Marcion note: the very name “Nazarene” was foretold, marking the Christ of the Creator. The stage is set: he stands up to read, and the eyes of his townsmen are upon him.

So the witnesses draw first the quiet lesson of the Lord’s habit. Barnes: “the Saviour regularly attended the service of the synagogue… there was great corruption… but Christ did not on that account keep away from the place of public worship” — condemning both the careless who neglect God’s house and the proud who forsake it for its faults. Lightfoot details the rite — the law read standing, leaning on nothing — and Wesley the posture, “to read standing, but to preach sitting.” Tertullian adds, against Marcion, that the very name “Nazarene” was foretold, marking the Christ of the Creator. The witnesses thus frame the great sermon with its homely setting and its high claim: the Son of God, in the town of his upbringing, keeps the sabbath as his custom was, stands up to read, and the eyes of his townsmen are upon him — the stage set for wonder and for offence.
17:
On Luke 4:17 — “And there was delivered unto him the book of the prophet Esaias. And when he had opened the book, he found the place where it was written” — the witnesses describe the synagogue lection.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the rite. Barnes and Lightfoot explain that the minister (the chazan) kept the sacred rolls and handed them to the reader; Robertson notes that on sabbaths some seven persons read portions, this being a lesson from the Prophets following the Law. Clarke describes the scroll “rolled on two rollers… unrolled” from one to the other; Ellicott infers that Isaiah “formed a roll by itself,” given separately into the Lord’s hand.

On whether Jesus chose the passage or lit upon it, the witnesses divide gently. Calvin holds that, “a liberty of choice” being allowed, “by his own judgment he took this passage in preference to others,” deliberately selecting it; Wesley leans the other way — “he found… opening upon it, by the particular providence of God.”

So the witnesses frame the moment with care for its homely truth and its high import. The scene is exactly that of a Jewish sabbath: the attendant brings the roll, the reader unrolls it and finds his place. Yet the place found is the great Messianic word of Isaiah, and the reader is its fulfilment. Whether by deliberate choice or by a providence that guided the unrolling, the passage could not have been more fitting; and the witnesses leave the small question open precisely because, either way, the hand of God is in it — the Anointed One opening the prophecy of the Anointing, in the synagogue of the town that had watched him grow.

So the witnesses frame the lection with care for its truth and its weight. The attendant brings the roll, the reader unrolls it (Clarke: “rolled on two rollers… unrolled”) and finds his place — the ordinary order of a Jewish sabbath. Yet the place found is the great Messianic word of Isaiah, and the reader its fulfilment. On whether he chose it or lit upon it the witnesses divide gently: Calvin holds he “deliberately selected this passage” by his own judgement, a liberty of choice being allowed; Wesley that “he found… opening upon it, by the particular providence of God.” The witnesses leave the small question open precisely because, either way, the hand of God is in it — the Anointed One opening the prophecy of the Anointing, in the synagogue of the town that had watched him grow, that the eyes now fastened on him might hear their own Scriptures fulfilled.
18:
On Luke 4:18 — “The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor… to preach deliverance to the captives” — the witnesses expound Isaiah’s great Messianic word as fulfilled in Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the text. Barnes: the passage “had a principal reference to the Messiah,” whom Christ “directly applies it to himself.” Robertson notes the verb “anointed” (echrisen), from which “Christ” the Anointed One is derived, applied here by Jesus to himself; the Spirit “upon me” as shown at the baptism (Luke 3:21). Clarke and Ellicott note Luke follows the Septuagint of Isaiah 61:1 (with a touch of Isaiah 42:7), and that the proclaiming of liberty alludes to the jubilee.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: these words teach that “both in his own person and in his ministers, Christ does not act by human authority… but has been sent by God,” doing all “by the guidance of the Spirit of God,” that “the faith of the godly may be founded on the authority and power of God.” Wesley marvels at the Trinity interwoven — “The Spirit of the Lord… hath anointed me” — and reads the gospel preached to the poor “literally and spiritually.”

So the witnesses hear, in the Lord’s chosen text, his own commission read aloud. The Anointed declares the Anointing: the Spirit rests on him, sending him to the poor, the broken, the captive, the blind. The very office of Messiah — to bring not judgement first but good news and release — is set out in Isaiah’s words and claimed by the One who reads them. And the witnesses mark the gracious shape of that office: it is to the poor and bound and bruised that the gospel comes, the year of jubilee proclaimed to those who have nothing and can do nothing — the very people the world overlooks, sought out by the Spirit-anointed Servant of the Lord.

So the witnesses hear, in the Lord’s chosen text, his own commission read aloud. The Anointed declares the Anointing: the Spirit rests on him, sending him to the poor, the broken, the captive, the blind, in the gracious shape of Messiah’s office — not judgement first but good news and release. Calvin draws the doctrine that Christ, “both in his own person and in his ministers… has been sent by God… by the guidance of the Spirit,” that faith may rest on God’s authority; Wesley marvels at the Trinity interwoven in the words. And the witnesses mark to whom the gospel comes: to the poor and bound and bruised, the very people the world overlooks. The jubilee is proclaimed to those who have nothing and can do nothing — sought out by the Spirit-anointed Servant, whose first word at Nazareth is not demand but glad tidings to the helpless.
19:
On Luke 4:19 — “To preach the acceptable year of the Lord” — the witnesses read the jubilee as fulfilled in the gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the figure. Barnes and Ellicott see “the year of jubilee” — the fiftieth year, when by trumpet the land returned to its owner, debts were forgiven, and Hebrew slaves freed (Leviticus 25:9–10) — as the type of “the ‘year’ of the divine kingdom,” the time “when God is willing to accept of men.” Robertson warns against the literalism of Clement and Origen, who inferred a ministry of only one year: “that is to turn figures into fact… The Messianic age has come.”

The Reformed Calvin reads it as the answer to a doubt: the prophet “makes the time of redemption to depend on the purpose, or good pleasure, of God,” lest weak minds be shaken by its long delay — “in an acceptable time have I heard thee.” Wesley: “plainly alluding to the year of jubilee, when all, both debtors and servants, were set free.”

So the witnesses gather the climax of the text. The whole sweep of Isaiah’s prophecy comes to rest on “the acceptable year” — the long-awaited season of grace, figured by Israel’s jubilee, now dawning in the person of the One who reads it. It is the year of release: the debt remitted, the slave freed, the inheritance restored — all the mercy that the jubilee trumpet proclaimed, now sounded in the gospel. And the witnesses guard the figure from a wooden literalism: the “year” is not a measure of months but the announcement that the time of God’s favour, long fixed in his good pleasure, has at last arrived in Christ.

So the witnesses gather the climax of the text. The whole sweep of Isaiah’s prophecy rests on “the acceptable year” — the long-awaited season of grace, figured by Israel’s jubilee, now dawning in the One who reads it. Barnes and Ellicott see the fiftieth year, when by trumpet the land returned, debts were forgiven, and slaves freed (Leviticus 25:9–10), as type of “the year of the divine kingdom.” Calvin reads it as the answer to the doubt bred by long delay: the time of redemption rests on God’s good pleasure. Robertson guards against the literalism that made it a one-year ministry: “the Messianic age has come.” The witnesses thus hear in “the acceptable year” not a measure of months but the announcement that the time of God’s favour, long fixed in his counsel, has at last arrived in Christ — the jubilee of debt remitted and captives freed sounded now in the gospel.
20:
On Luke 4:20 — “And he closed the book… and gave it again to the minister, and sat down. And the eyes of all… were fastened on him” — the witnesses mark the hush of expectation.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the gesture. Barnes: he “rolled it up again” and returned it to the minister whose office was to keep the books, then “sat down,” the usual Jewish posture for teaching. Robertson: he “took his seat there as a sign that he was going to speak,” not returning to his former place. Ellicott notes the calmness and deliberation, and that “the eyes of all… were fastened on him” (atenizontes, intently fixed, says Clarke).

The Reformed Calvin reads a divine working in the silence: “God touched their hearts… with astonishment, which made them more attentive,” so that they did not interrupt or break off the discourse, being for the moment held.

So the witnesses dwell on the charged stillness of the moment. The reading done, the roll handed back, the Lord sits — the teacher’s posture — and every eye in the synagogue is fixed on him. The very ordinariness of the actions, told with an eyewitness’s care, heightens the expectancy: the townsmen wait to hear what the carpenter’s son will make of the prophet’s word. And the witnesses see, even in their fixed attention, the hand of God arresting them — a hush that was itself a kind of grace, holding them to hear the gracious words about to fall, before their wonder should curdle, as it soon would, into offence.

So the witnesses dwell on the charged stillness. The reading done, the roll handed back, the Lord sits — the teacher’s posture (Robertson: “a sign that he was going to speak”) — and every eye in the synagogue is “fastened on him” (Clarke: atenizontes, intently fixed). Barnes and Ellicott note the calmness and deliberation of his actions, told with an eyewitness’s care, which only heighten the expectancy: the townsmen wait to hear what the carpenter’s son will make of the prophet’s word. And Calvin sees God’s own hand in the hush — he “touched their hearts… with astonishment, which made them more attentive” — a stillness that was itself a kind of grace, holding them to hear the gracious words about to fall, before their wonder should curdle, as it soon would, into the offence and fury that close the scene.
21:
On Luke 4:21 — “And he began to say unto them, This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears” — the witnesses mark the arresting opening of the sermon.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that only the text’s opening is preserved. Barnes: “it is probable that he said much more… but Luke has preserved only the substance” — the sum being, “this writing… is coming to pass; the thing originally intended by it is about to be accomplished.” Robertson reads the perfect “hath been fulfilled” (peplērōtai, “stands fulfilled”) and calls it “an arresting opening sentence.” Ellicott catches what startled them: “He had left them as the son of the carpenter… and now He came back” claiming the prophet’s word as fulfilled in himself.

The Reformed Calvin presses the method: Christ “did not merely affirm… but proved by a reference to facts, that the time was now come… just as expositors handle Scripture… when they apply it to the circumstances of those whom they address.”

So the witnesses fasten on the staggering claim of the opening word. “This day” — not in some distant age, but now, in their hearing — the great prophecy of the Anointed stands fulfilled. The One reading is the One foretold; the acceptable year has dawned in the man before them. It is, the witnesses agree, the crux of the whole scene: everything that follows — the wonder, the offence, the fury — turns on whether the townsmen will receive this. Christ does not merely expound Isaiah; he announces that Isaiah speaks of him, and that the long-promised time of grace has come to Nazareth in his person.

So the witnesses fasten on the staggering claim of the opening word. “This day” — not in some far age, but now, in their hearing — the great prophecy of the Anointed “stands fulfilled” (Robertson’s perfect, an “arresting opening sentence”). The One reading is the One foretold; the acceptable year has dawned in the man before them. Barnes notes only the substance is preserved — “he said much more” — and Ellicott catches what startled them: he had left as the carpenter’s son and returned claiming the prophet’s word as his own. Calvin marks the method — he “proved by a reference to facts that the time was now come.” The witnesses thus find the crux of the scene: everything that follows turns on whether the townsmen will receive this, that Isaiah speaks of him, and that the long-promised time of grace has come to Nazareth in his person.
22:
On Luke 4:22 — “And all bare him witness, and wondered at the gracious words… And they said, Is not this Joseph’s son?” — the witnesses read the wonder that already carried the seed of offence.

The Eastern and Latin fathers mark the grace and the envy. Chrysostom: at Nazareth he “refrains from miracles, lest He should provoke… greater malice,” but sets before them his teaching, “a certain ineffable grace… which softened the hearts of the hearers.” Ambrose warns that “in vain can you expect the aid of Divine mercy, if you grudge to others the fruits of their virtue.” The grammatical-historical commentators note Luke’s “words of grace” (Clarke: the “doctrines of grace”; Ellicott: the noun absent from Matthew and Mark, central later in Paul); Robertson reads the imperfect “bare witness” as inchoative — they began to admit the rumours were no exaggeration.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the turn: Luke shows “the truly divine grace which breathed in the lips of Christ,” and then “a lively picture of the ingratitude of men.” They are “compelled to acknowledge and admire God speaking in Christ; and yet they voluntarily refuse to render” the honour due. Wesley finds in all the Lord’s discourses “a peculiar sweetness, gravity, and becomingness.”

So the witnesses catch the hinge of the Nazareth scene. The townsmen cannot deny the grace of his words; they “wonder.” But in the same breath the fatal “Is not this Joseph’s son?” betrays the prejudice that will undo them. Their very familiarity with his origin, instead of leading them to faith, becomes the ground of contempt. The witnesses read here the tragedy of a wonder that does not ripen into trust: men may admire the beauty of Christ’s teaching and yet, stumbling at his lowliness, refuse him — admiration and unbelief lodged together in the same heart.

So the witnesses catch the hinge of the Nazareth scene. The townsmen cannot deny the grace of his words and “wonder”; Chrysostom marks the “ineffable grace… which softened the hearts of the hearers.” But in the same breath the fatal “Is not this Joseph’s son?” betrays the prejudice that will undo them. Calvin sets the two together — “the truly divine grace which breathed in the lips of Christ,” and then “a lively picture of the ingratitude of men.” Origen reads a deeper figure: Nazareth a type of the unbelieving Jews, Capernaum of the Gentiles who would receive him. The witnesses thus read the tragedy of a wonder that does not ripen into trust: men may admire the beauty of Christ’s teaching and yet, stumbling at his lowliness, refuse him — admiration and unbelief lodged together in the same heart, the very seed of the wrath soon to break out.
23:
On Luke 4:23 — “Ye will surely say unto me this proverb, Physician, heal thyself… whatsoever we have heard done in Capernaum, do also here” — the witnesses read the Lord’s reading of their hearts.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the proverb. Barnes: “suppose that a man should attempt to heal another when he was himself diseased… You profess to be the Messiah… work here the miracles we hear of elsewhere.” Robertson notes the physician “was expected to take his own medicine,” and that Luke the physician alone preserves the saying; Ellicott, “the beloved physician,” finds it fitting he should record it. Clarke: heal “the broken-hearted in thy own country” — but they were not in a spirit to receive it.

The Reformed Calvin marks that Christ “states publicly those thoughts which he knew to exist in their minds,” and so “imputes to them the blame of his declining to work miracles among them,” charging their malice. Wesley reads their unspoken demand: “Why does not my love begin at home? Why do I not work miracles here, rather than at Capernaum?”

So the witnesses see the Lord answer not their words but their hearts. He knows the proverb already forming on their lips: prove yourself to us, your own townsmen; do here the wonders rumoured of Capernaum. Beneath the demand lies the prejudice that, knowing his family, will not believe without a sign extorted on their terms. And the witnesses mark the justice of his reply: it was their unbelief, not any want in him, that withheld his works from Nazareth. The Physician would not be dictated to by the patients who despised him; the very demand for proof, springing from contempt, was the reason proof was denied.

So the witnesses see the Lord answer not their words but their hearts. He knows the proverb forming on their lips — prove yourself to us, your own townsmen; do here the wonders rumoured of Capernaum. Robertson notes the physician “was expected to take his own medicine,” and that Luke the physician alone preserves the saying. Beneath the demand, Calvin marks, lies a malice that, knowing his family, will not believe without a sign extorted on their own terms; so Christ “imputes to them the blame of his declining to work miracles among them.” The witnesses thus find the justice of his reply: it was their unbelief, not any want in him, that withheld his works from Nazareth. The Physician would not be dictated to by patients who despised him; the very demand for proof, springing from contempt, was the reason proof was denied.
24:
On Luke 4:24 — “And he said, Verily I say unto you, No prophet is accepted in his own country” — the witnesses hear the proverb of the dishonoured prophet.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the saying and its frequency. Ellicott notes it was quoted “certainly twice, possibly oftener” — here, at the second Nazareth visit (Matthew 13:57; Mark 6:4), and echoed in John 4:44 — conveying “the impression of the saying having been often on the lips of Jesus.” Robertson notes the solemn introduction, “Verily I say unto you,” and that Jesus had already applied it to himself.

The Reformed Calvin reads it of their unbelief blocking his power: “the unbelief of men presents an obstruction to God, and hinders him from working” — not that God’s power is bound, but that he withholds, as a just rebuke, where faith is wanting (Mark 6:5). Wesley explains the proverb’s sense: “the meanness of his family, or lowness of his circumstances, bring his office into contempt.”

So the witnesses set the Nazareth rejection within a known and melancholy pattern. It is the common lot of God’s messengers to be least honoured where they are best known; familiarity, which should breed trust, breeds instead a contempt that cannot believe the neighbour to be the prophet. Christ, claiming this proverb, sets himself among the prophets so treated, and warns Nazareth that their unbelief is no new thing under the sun. The verse thus both explains their offence and condemns it: the prophet is not less a prophet for being dishonoured at home, and the dishonour falls back, not on him, but on those too proud to receive him.

So the witnesses set the rejection within a known and melancholy pattern. Ellicott notes the proverb was on the Lord’s lips “often” (Matthew 13:57; Mark 6:4; John 4:44): no prophet is honoured at home, for familiarity, which should breed trust, breeds contempt of the neighbour. Calvin reads it of their unbelief blocking his power — “the unbelief of men presents an obstruction to God” — not that his power is bound, but that he withholds, as a just rebuke, where faith is wanting (Mark 6:5). Wesley: the meanness of his family “bring his office into contempt.” The witnesses thus see Christ set himself among the prophets so treated, warning Nazareth that their offence is no new thing under the sun — and that the dishonour falls back not on him but on those too proud to receive him.
25:
On Luke 4:25 — “But I tell you of a truth, many widows were in Israel in the days of Elias, when the heaven was shut up three years and six months” — the witnesses read the first of two Old Testament warnings.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the case. Barnes: Christ “calls attention to two cases where acknowledged prophets… bestowed their favours on foreigners,” so “such is the want of faith in my own country” that the evidence will go to others. Robertson and Lightfoot note the “three years and six months” (so James 5:17), the popular Jewish reckoning of the drought, though 1 Kings 18:1 dates the rain “in the third year”; Wesley reconciles by counting from Elijah’s sojourn at Sarepta.

The Reformed Calvin draws the sting: by these examples Christ shows “that they ought not to think it strange, if God prefers strangers to the inhabitants of the country,” and throws “an indirect hint as to their vanity and presumption.”

So the witnesses see the Lord turn from proverb to history, and from defence to warning. The famine of Elijah’s day is the backdrop: many widows hungered in Israel, yet the prophet was sent to none of them. The lesson is pointed at Nazareth’s pride. They demanded miracles as their due, as the prophet’s own townsmen; Christ answers that God is not bound to his unbelieving countrymen, and has before passed them by to bless the stranger. The verse opens the theme that will rouse their fury — that the grace they presume upon may be carried, over their unbelieving heads, to the Gentiles.

So the witnesses see the Lord turn from proverb to history, and from defence to warning. The famine of Elijah’s day is the backdrop — the heaven shut “three years and six months” (so James 5:17, the popular reckoning; cf. 1 Kings 18:1) — when many widows hungered in Israel. Barnes: Christ “calls attention to two cases where acknowledged prophets… bestowed their favours on foreigners,” so such is the unbelief of his own country that the evidence will go to others. Calvin draws the sting: God may “prefer strangers to the inhabitants of the country,” an indirect rebuke of their “vanity and presumption.” The witnesses thus see the theme opened that will rouse their fury — that the grace they presume upon as Christ’s townsmen may be carried, over their unbelieving heads, to the stranger, as once it was in the days of the prophets.
26:
On Luke 4:26 — “But unto none of them was Elias sent, save unto Sarepta, a city of Sidon, unto a woman that was a widow” — the witnesses mark that the prophet was sent to a Gentile.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the point. Barnes: Sarepta “was not a Jewish city, but a Sidonian, and therefore a Gentile town”; “he was sent to none of the widows in Israel,” but to this foreign woman (1 Kings 17:9). Clarke and Ellicott press the force of the Greek: better “but unto Sarepta,” the conjunction marking “a contrast rather than an exception” — the widow was a Sidonian, no widow of Israel at all. Robertson notes she was “in the land of Sidon or Phoenicia, a heathen, where Jesus himself will go later.”

The verse drives the warning home with a single, undeniable instance. In all the famine of Israel, the great prophet Elijah was sustained not by, nor sent to relieve, any daughter of his own nation, but a heathen widow of Sidon. The favour of God, in that day, passed over unbelieving Israel and lighted on a Gentile.

So the witnesses read in the Sidonian widow a standing rebuke to national presumption and a quiet foreshadowing of the gospel’s course. Ellicott marks it “a striking instance of His method of reading the underlying lessons of the Old Testament”; the same Lord who notes that Elijah was sent to a Gentile will himself, the witnesses observe, go into the borders of Sidon and find faith there. The lesson Nazareth could not bear was the very pattern of grace: that God bestows his mercy where he finds faith, not where men claim it by birth, and that a believing stranger is dearer to him than an unbelieving son of the covenant.

So the witnesses drive the warning home with a single, undeniable instance. Barnes: Sarepta “was not a Jewish city, but a Sidonian, and therefore a Gentile town” (1 Kings 17:9); Clarke and Ellicott press the Greek — “but unto Sarepta,” marking “a contrast rather than an exception” — the widow was no widow of Israel at all. In all the famine of Israel the great prophet was sustained by, and sent to, a heathen woman of Sidon. Ellicott calls it “a striking instance of His method of reading the underlying lessons of the Old Testament.” The witnesses thus find in the Sidonian widow a rebuke to national presumption and a foreshadowing of the gospel’s course: God bestows his mercy where he finds faith, not where men claim it by birth, and a believing stranger is dearer to him than an unbelieving son of the covenant.
27:
On Luke 4:27 — “And many lepers were in Israel in the time of Eliseus the prophet; and none of them was cleansed, saving Naaman the Syrian” — the witnesses read the second warning, matched to the first.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the case beside the former. Barnes and Robertson note the cleansing of Naaman, the lone leper healed by Elisha (2 Kings 5:1, 14), a Syrian and a heathen; Ellicott again marks the Greek — “but Naaman” — contrast, not exception. Lightfoot observes how these instances “galled those of Nazareth”: they saw themselves “vilified by these examples,” reckoned, like unbelieving Israel of old, unworthy of the prophets’ favour.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the doctrine of sovereign mercy: “God dispenses his benefits when, where, and to whom he pleases. No person can complain… because no person deserves any good from his hand.”

So the witnesses see the second example seal the first. As Elijah was sent to a Sidonian widow, so Elisha cleansed a Syrian leper, while the many lepers of Israel went uncleansed. Two prophets, two Gentiles, one lesson: the grace of God is not the entitlement of a nation but the gift of a sovereign hand, and it lights where faith receives it. The witnesses mark how exactly the cases met Nazareth’s case — they too had lepers and widows of need at home, and they too, in unbelief, would see the Physician’s power go elsewhere. The warning was plain, and it galled them: the favour they presumed upon as Christ’s townsmen might, like Elijah’s and Elisha’s, be carried to the believing stranger.

So the witnesses see the second example seal the first. As Elijah was sent to a Sidonian widow, so Elisha cleansed a Syrian leper — Naaman, the lone leper healed while the many lepers of Israel went uncleansed (2 Kings 5:1, 14); again Ellicott marks the Greek, “but Naaman,” contrast not exception. Clarke draws the doctrine of sovereign mercy: “God dispenses his benefits when, where, and to whom he pleases. No person can complain… because no person deserves any good from his hand.” Lightfoot notes how these instances “galled those of Nazareth,” who saw themselves vilified like unbelieving Israel of old. The witnesses thus read two prophets, two Gentiles, one lesson: the grace of God is not a nation’s entitlement but a sovereign gift, lighting where faith receives it — and the favour Nazareth presumed upon might, like Elijah’s and Elisha’s, be carried to the believing stranger.
28:
On Luke 4:28 — “And all they in the synagogue, when they heard these things, were filled with wrath” — the witnesses mark the sudden turn from wonder to fury.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the cause. Cyril: “because He had said, This day is this prophecy fulfilled, they thought that He compared Himself to the prophets, and are therefore enraged.” Ambrose: “it cannot be wondered at that they lost their salvation who cast the Saviour out of their city.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “the people of Nazareth at once caught on and saw the point of these two… illustrations of how God… blessed the heathen instead of the Jewish people”; Barnes lists their reasons — the cases applied to themselves, he turned to others, and the Gentiles were made objects of God’s compassion.

The Reformed Calvin: they perceived that the examples meant “that the grace of God would be removed from them to others,” and so “considered that he had spoken to their dishonour” — yet, instead of being “stung to the quick” with repentance, they flew into rage. Wesley: “So changeable are the hearts of wicked men!”

So the witnesses watch the synagogue’s mood overturn in a moment. The same men who had “wondered at the gracious words” are now “filled with wrath.” What enraged them was not error but truth — the intolerable suggestion that God’s grace might pass them by for the Gentiles, that a faith found in Sidon and Syria was wanting in Israel. The witnesses read here a solemn lesson: the preaching that begins in admiration may end in fury when it touches the hearer’s pride, and the surest way to provoke an unbelieving heart is to tell it the truth about itself. Their wrath was the measure of their unbelief.

So the witnesses watch the synagogue’s mood overturn in a moment. The men who had “wondered at the gracious words” are now “filled with wrath.” Cyril: because he said the prophecy was fulfilled, “they thought that He compared Himself to the prophets, and are therefore enraged.” Robertson notes they “saw the point” of the two illustrations — God blessing the heathen instead of the Jews; Calvin, that they perceived “the grace of God would be removed from them to others,” and, instead of being “stung to the quick” with repentance, raged. Wesley: “So changeable are the hearts of wicked men!” The witnesses thus read a solemn lesson: what enraged them was not error but truth — the intolerable suggestion that a faith found in Sidon and Syria was wanting in Israel. The preaching that began in admiration ends in fury when it touches the hearer’s pride; their wrath was the measure of their unbelief.
29:
On Luke 4:29 — “And rose up, and thrust him out of the city, and led him unto the brow of the hill… that they might cast him down headlong” — the witnesses mark the murderous violence of the mob.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the scene. Barnes: the region was hilly, and they led him to a precipice — “this was the effect of a popular tumult… they had no legal right to take life.” Robertson reads the ingressive aorists, “a movement towards lynching Jesus,” to the “brow” (ophrys, eyebrow) of the hill. Lightfoot notes they had no lawful power in capital matters, so this was mob fury, not process; Ellicott observes that casting down headlong “was not a recognised Jewish punishment.”

The verse shows how far and how fast the synagogue’s wrath ran. Rising in a tumult, before the service was well over, they hustle the Lord out of the town and toward a cliff, meaning to fling him to his death. The men who had just admired his gracious words now seek his life; admiration soured into rage, and rage into attempted murder, in the space of a single sermon.

So the witnesses read in the rush to the brow of the hill the true nature of unbelief when it is crossed. It was no orderly judgement but a lawless lynching, the fury of a pride that could not bear the truth. And the witnesses mark the irony: the townsmen who would not have him as prophet would have him as victim, hurried to the edge of a precipice in the very town of his upbringing. The scene anticipates the cross — the rejected Christ thrust out of the city — and shows, even at the threshold of his ministry, the murderous end to which the world’s offended unbelief would drive.

So the witnesses read in the rush to the brow of the hill the true nature of unbelief when it is crossed. Rising in a tumult, before the service was well over, they hustle the Lord out of the town toward a precipice, meaning to fling him to his death. Barnes notes it was “the effect of a popular tumult… they had no legal right to take life”; Lightfoot, that the local court had no power in capital matters; Ellicott, that casting headlong “was not a recognised Jewish punishment.” It was no orderly judgement but a lawless lynching, the fury of a pride that could not bear the truth. The witnesses mark the irony — the townsmen who would not have him as prophet would have him as victim — and see the scene anticipate the cross: the rejected Christ thrust out of the city, the murderous end to which offended unbelief would drive.
30:
On Luke 4:30 — “But he passing through the midst of them went his way” — the witnesses divide gently over the manner of the escape.

Most read it as a miracle. The Reformed Calvin: “God rescued him, by an extraordinary miracle, from immediate death” — teaching that “the power of God will always be victorious to preserve us, so long as he shall be pleased to keep us.” Gill suggests he “made use of, and showed his divine power,” whether holding their eyes or passing unseen. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: “he shut their eyes… or so overawed them… The man Christ Jesus was immortal till his time came”; Wesley: “perhaps invisibly; or perhaps they were overawed.” Barnes notes the parallel escape at John 8:59.

Against this, the grammatical-historical Ellicott cautions: the words “do not necessarily involve a directly supernatural deliverance… We have no right to insert miracles in the Gospel records. Calmness, silence, the moral power of self-possessed righteousness have in themselves a power… to baffle the fury of an angry mob.” Robertson reads simply the imperfect, “he was going on his way.”

So the witnesses, whatever their explanation, agree on the substance: at the very edge of the precipice the Lord passes untouched through the midst of his enemies and goes calmly on. Whether by a miracle that blinded or overawed them, or by the sheer moral majesty of a self-possessed righteousness before which the mob fell back, the deliverance is real and the lesson one: “his time was not yet come.” The witnesses find here the truth that the Lord — and every servant of his — is immortal till his work is done, and that no fury of men can take the life that God is pleased to keep until the appointed hour.

So the witnesses, whatever their explanation, agree on the substance: at the very edge of the precipice the Lord passes untouched through the midst of his enemies and goes calmly on. Calvin and Gill, Clarke and Wesley read a miracle — God rescuing him “by an extraordinary miracle,” whether by holding their eyes or overawing them, for “the man Christ Jesus was immortal till his time came” (cf. John 8:59). Ellicott cautions against inserting a miracle where the text does not require one: “the moral power of self-possessed righteousness” can of itself “baffle the fury of an angry mob.” The witnesses leave the manner open but draw one lesson: “his time was not yet come.” The Lord — and every servant of his — is immortal till his work is done, and no fury of men can take the life that God is pleased to keep until the appointed hour.
31:
On Luke 4:31 — “And came down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and taught them on the sabbath days” — the witnesses mark the move to the new headquarters of the ministry.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the Lord’s persevering mercy. Ambrose: “neither indignation… nor displeasure at their wickedness caused our Lord to abandon Judea, but… remembering mercy… He softens the hearts of this unbelieving people.” Bede: “the word of the teacher is with power, when he performs that which he teaches”; Cyril: he “intermingles with His teaching the performance of mighty works,” for those whose reason will not incline are roused by miracles. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Capernaum is now “the headquarters of the Galilean ministry, since Nazareth has rejected Jesus,” and that Luke here parallels Mark 1:21; Ellicott marks that Luke, unlike Matthew, gives “the reason of the removal.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “He came down to Capernaum, and dwelt there, entirely quitting his abode at Nazareth.” The Latin Tertullian adds his anti-Marcion polemic, fastening on Capernaum to refute Marcion’s phantom-Christ.

So the witnesses set the scene of the Capernaum ministry against the dark backdrop of Nazareth’s rejection. Cast out by his own town, the Lord does not retire in resentment but goes down to Capernaum and resumes his teaching, “remembering mercy.” There his word is “with power” — the authority that astonished his hearers — and there, the fathers note, he joins miracle to teaching, that those unmoved by doctrine might be roused by works. The verse opens the section of Capernaum wonders, and shows the rejected Prophet carrying his unwearied grace to a new city, where the demoniac in the synagogue will furnish the first proof of his power.

So the witnesses set the Capernaum ministry against the dark backdrop of Nazareth’s rejection. Cast out by his own town, the Lord does not retire in resentment but, Ambrose says, “remembering mercy… softens the hearts of this unbelieving people.” Bede: “the word of the teacher is with power, when he performs that which he teaches”; Cyril: he “intermingles with His teaching the performance of mighty works,” that those unmoved by doctrine might be roused by miracles. Robertson notes Capernaum is now “the headquarters of the Galilean ministry, since Nazareth has rejected Jesus,” and Ellicott that Luke alone gives the reason for the removal. The witnesses thus see the rejected Prophet carrying his unwearied grace to a new city, his word “with power,” where the demoniac in the synagogue will furnish the first proof — mercy pressing on past the place that would not have it.
32:
On Luke 4:32 — “And they were astonished at his doctrine: for his word was with power” — the witnesses mark the authority that set Christ apart from the scribes.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the “power.” Clarke: it was “with authority” (en exousia) — he “assumed the tone and manner of a new Lawgiver,” uttering his doctrines “not in the way of exhortation or advice, but in the form of precepts and commands, the unction of the Holy Spirit accompanying all he said.” Ellicott: the word is the same as the “authority” of Matthew 7:29 — “no timid references to the traditions of the elders… such as they were familiar with.” Robertson notes Luke omits Mark’s “and not as their scribes,” but the sense is the same.

The astonishment of Capernaum, like the wonder of Nazareth, fastened on the manner of his teaching. The scribes taught by citing authorities, hedging every saying with the opinions of the elders; Christ taught as one who needed no such props, speaking with the direct authority of God. His word carried its own power, and the hearers felt it.

So the witnesses mark the difference between the Lord and all merely human teachers. His doctrine was not borrowed but original, not tentative but commanding, not the echo of tradition but the voice of the Lawgiver himself. Behind it lay “the unction of the Holy Spirit” with which he had returned from the wilderness in power. The astonishment of the people was the natural response to a thing they had never heard in their synagogues: a man who spoke of God’s things as one who knew them from within, and whose word, the next verse will show, had power not only to instruct but to command the very devils.

So the witnesses mark the difference between the Lord and all merely human teachers. Clarke: his word was “with authority” — he “assumed the tone and manner of a new Lawgiver,” speaking “not in the way of exhortation… but in the form of precepts and commands, the unction of the Holy Spirit accompanying all he said”; Ellicott, that there were “no timid references to the traditions of the elders” such as filled the scribes’ sermons. The scribes taught by citing authorities; Christ taught as one who needed none, with the direct authority of God, and the hearers felt it. The witnesses thus see the astonishment of Capernaum fasten rightly on the manner of his teaching — a man who spoke of God’s things as one who knew them from within, whose word, the next verse shows, had power not only to instruct but to command the very devils.
33:
On Luke 4:33 — “And in the synagogue there was a man, which had a spirit of an unclean devil” — the witnesses note the first recorded exorcism and Luke’s exact language.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the phrase. Clarke notes that “the inspired writers never use the word daimon in a good sense,” and that “unclean” here marks the quality of the spirit. Robertson observes Luke’s unique combination, “a spirit of an unclean demon,” and the physician-evangelist’s precise usage. Lightfoot ponders whether it points to a “pythonic or necromantic spirit.”

The Reformed Calvin reads the man as one wholly driven: “by the permission of God, Satan had seized the faculties of his soul… to drive him not only to speak, but to perform other movements, at his pleasure” — so that “the devils, who have received permission to tyrannise, speak in them and by them.”

So the witnesses set the scene of the Capernaum miracle. Into the synagogue, where the Lord taught with authority, comes a man possessed — a soul under the tyranny of an unclean spirit. The astonishment at his teaching is about to be sealed by a deed: the word that commanded attention will command the demon.
