The witnesses mark the reality of the bondage, against every attempt to reduce it to mere disease; here is a true usurpation of a human soul by a malignant power, permitted by God for a season — and into that bondage the Lord, full of the Spirit and victor over the tempter in the wilderness, now brings the liberating authority of his word.

So the witnesses set the scene of the first recorded exorcism. Into the synagogue, where the Lord taught with authority, comes a man possessed — a soul under the tyranny of an unclean spirit. Clarke notes the inspired writers never use “demon” in a good sense; Robertson, Luke’s exact phrase “a spirit of an unclean demon”; Lightfoot ponders a “pythonic” spirit. Calvin reads the man as wholly driven: “Satan had seized the faculties of his soul… to drive him… at his pleasure,” the devils speaking “in them and by them.” The witnesses thus mark the reality of the bondage, against every attempt to reduce it to mere disease: here is a true usurpation of a human soul by a malignant power, permitted by God for a season — and into that bondage the Lord, victor over the tempter in the wilderness, now brings the liberating authority of his word.
34:
On Luke 4:34 — “Let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus of Nazareth?… I know thee who thou art; the Holy One of God” — the witnesses weigh the demon’s cry and its confession.

The grammatical-historical Robertson reads the opening interjection “Ah!” (Ea) as “a diabolical screech” of wonder, fear, and indignation. Barnes points the title “the Holy One of God” to its Scriptural background (Psalm 16:10; Daniel 9:24). The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the lesson of devilish knowledge: the demon’s confession was “extorted… by terror, for the devils believe and tremble”; the spirits know Christ better, in a sense, than the men who reject him.

The cry is a mixture of dread and defiance. The unclean spirit recognises in the carpenter of Nazareth “the Holy One of God,” and shrinks — “what have we to do with thee?” — fearing destruction. Where the townsmen of Nazareth saw only Joseph’s son, the demon sees the Holy One; the powers of darkness confess what unbelief denies.

So the witnesses find a solemn irony in the scene. The first clear confession of Christ’s holiness and divine sonship in this Capernaum synagogue comes not from a worshipper but from a devil — truth wrung out by terror. It is the standing testimony of the New Testament that “the devils also believe, and tremble”: a knowledge of who Christ is, however accurate, is not faith, and may consist with the deepest enmity. The demon’s cry both honours the Lord and condemns the unbelief of men, who had every reason to confess what the unclean spirit was forced to own; and the Lord, as the next verse shows, will not accept the testimony of such a witness.

So the witnesses dwell on the irony of the demon’s cry. Robertson reads the opening “Ah!” (Ea) as “a diabolical screech” of fear and defiance; the spirit recognises in the carpenter of Nazareth “the Holy One of God” (Psalm 16:10) and shrinks. Where the townsmen saw only Joseph’s son, the demon sees the Holy One; the powers of darkness confess what unbelief denies. Wesley marks the confession “extorted… by terror, for the devils believe and tremble.” The witnesses thus find a solemn lesson: the first clear confession of Christ’s holiness in this synagogue comes not from a worshipper but from a devil, truth wrung out by dread. A knowledge of who Christ is, however accurate, is not faith, and may consist with the deepest enmity; the demon’s cry both honours the Lord and condemns the men who had every reason to confess what the unclean spirit was forced to own.
35:
On Luke 4:35 — “And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him. And when the devil had thrown him in the midst, he came out of him, and hurt him not” — the witnesses mark the authority of the word and the physician’s care.

The grammatical-historical commentators note Luke’s exactness. Robertson observes the violent verb “had thrown him down in the midst” (rhipsan, to hurl), and that Luke the physician “carefully notes” the detail not in Mark — “having done him no hurt” (blapsan, a medical word, here only and in Mark 16:18). Clarke: though the spirit “convulsed him… and threw him down… probably with the design to take away his life, yet our Lord permitted it not.” Gill marks that Jesus rebuked “not the man, but the unclean spirit.”

The command is twofold and absolute: “Hold thy peace, and come out.” The Lord silences the demon’s confession and casts it out in a word. And Luke, with a physician’s eye, records that for all the spirit’s final violence the man was unharmed — the malignant power, that would have destroyed its victim on the way out, was restrained from doing him any real injury.

So the witnesses behold the authority foretold by the people’s astonishment now displayed in act. The word that taught with power now commands the unclean spirit, and is instantly obeyed. Two things they mark: that Christ would not have his testimony from devils, silencing the spirit even as he expelled it; and that his power is joined to tenderness, suffering the demon to convulse but not to harm. The same Lord who rebukes the powers of darkness guards the soul they had oppressed — casting out the tyrant and leaving the man whole.

So the witnesses behold the authority foretold by the people’s astonishment now displayed in act. The command is twofold and absolute — “Hold thy peace, and come out” — and instantly obeyed. Gill marks that Jesus rebuked “not the man, but the unclean spirit”; Robertson, the violent verb (the spirit “hurled him down”) and Luke the physician’s careful note “having done him no hurt” (Mark 16:18 the only other use of the medical word). Clarke: though it convulsed him, “probably with the design to take away his life, yet our Lord permitted it not.” The witnesses thus mark two things: that Christ would not have his testimony from devils, silencing the spirit even as he expelled it; and that his power is joined to tenderness, suffering the demon to convulse but not to harm — casting out the tyrant and leaving the man whole.
36:
On Luke 4:36 — “And they were all amazed… saying, What a word is this! for with authority and power he commandeth the unclean spirits, and they come out” — the witnesses mark the people’s wondering acknowledgement.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect and the reciprocal — “they spake together one with another” — and the ambiguous “for” (hoti), which may be “that” or “because.” The Reformed Calvin reads the result: “they are compelled to acknowledge that there is in Christ something more than man, and justly trace the glory and power of the miracle to his doctrine” — the word which even the devils obey.

The amazement of Capernaum now joins what it had separated: the authority of the teaching and the power of the deed. The people had wondered at his doctrine; now they see that the same word which commanded their attention commands the unclean spirits, and is obeyed. Their question — “What a word is this!” — fastens, rightly, on the word as the seat of the power.

So the witnesses find the miracle interpreting the teaching. The people do not merely marvel at a wonder; they trace the wonder to the word, and the word to a power “more than man.” The exorcism is thus the seal of the doctrine: the authority they had felt in his speaking is proved divine when the demons flee at his command. Calvin’s point stands — the right response to the miracle is to honour the doctrine it confirms; and the witnesses see in Capernaum’s astonishment the dawning recognition, however imperfect, that in this teacher God himself had drawn near, speaking and acting with a power the powers of darkness could not resist.

So the witnesses find the miracle interpreting the teaching. The people do not merely marvel at a wonder; they trace it to the word — “What a word is this!” — and the word to a power “more than man.” Robertson notes the reciprocal “they spake one with another” and the ambiguous “for”; Calvin, that “they are compelled to acknowledge that there is in Christ something more than man, and justly trace the glory and power of the miracle to his doctrine” — the word which even the devils obey. The exorcism is thus the seal of the doctrine: the authority they had felt in his speaking is proved divine when the demons flee at his command. The witnesses see in Capernaum’s astonishment the dawning recognition, however imperfect, that in this teacher God himself had drawn near, speaking and acting with a power the powers of darkness could not resist.
37:
On Luke 4:37 — “And the fame of him went out into every place of the country round about” — the witnesses mark the spreading report of the Capernaum miracle.

The voices here are few, and dwell on the vivid word. Clarke calls “the fame” (ēchos, a sound) “a very elegant metaphor”: the people are “struck with astonishment, and the sound goes out… in allusion to the propagation of sound… through all the coasts.” Robertson agrees — “our very word echo in this word” — an imperfect that “kept on going forth,” a “vivid picture of the resounding influence of this day’s work.” Gill notes it “seems to be the first instance of this kind,” the first recorded casting out of an unclean spirit.

The verse is the natural sequel to the wonder. What the synagogue saw, the country soon heard; the report of the miracle rang out like a sound carried on the air, reaching “every place… round about.” The fame of the new Teacher, already great for his doctrine, now swelled with the news of his power over the spirits.

So the witnesses find in the spreading “echo” both the providence and the peril of fame. The report would draw the multitudes that the next verses describe, bringing their sick to be healed; the day’s work in the synagogue resounded far. Yet the witnesses elsewhere mark how fickle such fame can be — the same crowds that gather for wonders may melt when the demands of the gospel are pressed. For now, the verse simply records the irrepressible spread of the news: the authority of Christ’s word could not be hidden, and the sound of it went out through all Galilee.

So the witnesses find in the spreading “echo” both the providence and the peril of fame. Clarke calls the word (ēchos, a sound) “a very elegant metaphor” — the report ringing out “in allusion to the propagation of sound… through all the coasts”; Robertson, an imperfect that “kept on going forth,” a “vivid picture of the resounding influence of this day’s work.” The report would draw the multitudes the next verses describe, bringing their sick to be healed. Yet the witnesses elsewhere mark how fickle such fame can be — the crowds that gather for wonders may melt when the gospel’s demands are pressed. For now the verse simply records the irrepressible spread of the news: the authority of Christ’s word could not be hidden, and the sound of it went out, like an echo carried on the air, through all the country round about Capernaum.
38:
On Luke 4:38 — “And he arose out of the synagogue, and entered into Simon’s house. And Simon’s wife’s mother was taken with a great fever; and they besought him for her” — the witnesses move from the synagogue to the home.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the order and the honour. Ambrose: Luke, “having first introduced a man delivered from an evil spirit, goes on to relate the healing of a woman. For our Lord had come to heal each sex.” Chrysostom: he “honoured His disciples by dwelling among them.” Bede: “at one time at the request of others, at another of his own accord, our Saviour cures the sick,” ever granting “the prayer of the faithful.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “the house of Simon and Andrew” (Mark 1:29), and that Luke the physician calls it a “great fever.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the domestic lesson: “as soon as Peter began to follow Christ, his family began to benefit by it… One person full of faith and prayer may be the means of drawing down innumerable blessings on his family.”

So the witnesses follow the Lord from the place of public worship to the privacy of a disciple’s home, and find his grace as ready there as in the synagogue. The same authority that cast out the demon now stoops to a sick woman’s bedside, for he “had come to heal each sex” and every need. The fathers mark the honour done to Peter’s house, and Clarke the blessing that follows discipleship into the family. The scene teaches that Christ’s power is not reserved for the great and public occasions: he enters the ordinary household, hears the prayer made on a relative’s behalf, and brings his healing to the common troubles of a home.

So the witnesses follow the Lord from public worship to a disciple’s home, and find his grace as ready there as in the synagogue. Ambrose: “our Lord had come to heal each sex,” and so, having delivered a man, he heals a woman; Bede, that he cures “at the request of others” or “of his own accord,” ever granting “the prayer of the faithful.” Robertson notes “the house of Simon and Andrew” (Mark 1:29) and the physician’s “great fever”; Clarke draws the domestic lesson — “as soon as Peter began to follow Christ, his family began to benefit by it.” The witnesses thus see that Christ’s power is not reserved for great public occasions: the same authority that cast out the demon stoops to a sick woman’s bedside, hears the prayer made on her behalf, and brings his healing to the common troubles of a home.
39:
On Luke 4:39 — “And he stood over her, and rebuked the fever; and it left her: and immediately she arose and ministered unto them” — the witnesses mark the manner and completeness of the cure.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “he stood over her” (only in Luke), the attitude of “any kindly sympathetic physician,” and “rebuked” the fever as he had the demon. Origen reads the figure of speech — “as of a command given to a sensible being” — the fever “commanded” and obeying. Chrysostom marks the proof of power in the manner of the cure: “after the allaying of the fever, the patient needs much time ere he be restored” — but she arose at once and ministered, doing “what nature could never do.”

The Reformed Calvin explains the “rebuke”: diseases, like famine and pestilence, are “God’s heralds… by whom he executes his judgments,” sent and restrained at his command; so the Lord, rebuking the fever, speaks as its sovereign.

So the witnesses find the cure doubly remarkable — in its means and in its effect. He healed not with medicines but with a word of command, treating the fever as a thing subject to his rule; and the healing was instantaneous and entire, the woman rising not weak and convalescent but strong enough at once to serve. The witnesses read in her immediate ministering both the proof of the miracle and its proper fruit: health restored by Christ is health rendered back to him in service. The same hand that lifted her from her bed enlisted her, that very hour, in glad and grateful work for him and his.

So the witnesses find the cure doubly remarkable — in its means and in its effect. Robertson notes “he stood over her” (only in Luke), the attitude of “any kindly sympathetic physician,” and “rebuked” the fever as he had the demon; Origen reads the command given “as to a sensible being”; Chrysostom marks the proof of power — where a healed patient “needs much time ere he be restored,” she rose at once and ministered. Calvin: diseases are “God’s heralds… by whom he executes his judgments,” sent and restrained at his command, so Christ rebukes the fever as its sovereign. The witnesses thus read in her immediate ministering both the proof of the miracle and its proper fruit: health restored by Christ is health rendered back to him in service — the hand that lifted her from her bed enlisting her, that very hour, in glad and grateful work.
40:
On Luke 4:40 — “Now when the sun was setting, all they that had any sick… brought them unto him; and he laid his hands on every one of them, and healed them” — the witnesses mark the eventide gathering of the afflicted.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the timing. Clarke and Wesley: “when the sun was set… the Sabbath ended” — reckoned from sunset to sunset — and only then was it held lawful to carry the sick (John 5:10). Robertson notes the genitive absolute, “the sun setting,” a vivid sunset scene, and that the news of the synagogue cure had spread.

The Eastern fathers mark the people’s zeal and its limits. Theophylact: “we must observe the zeal of the multitude, who after the sun had set bring their sick to Him, not deterred by the lateness of the day”; Origen notes they waited for sunset because, by their reckoning, it was “not lawful” to carry the sick on the sabbath.

So the witnesses picture the close of that crowded sabbath. As the sun goes down and the sabbath ends, the whole town streams to Peter’s door with its sick, and the Lord, untiring, lays his hands on “every one of them” and heals them all. The witnesses mark both the people’s eagerness — held back by scruple only till the legal hour — and the Lord’s individual care: not a general word over the crowd, but a hand laid on each. The verse shows the abundance of his compassion and power, healing all who came; and it shows the fitting end of the sabbath of teaching and deliverance, the day of rest closing in a flood of mercy poured out on the afflicted of Capernaum.

So the witnesses picture the close of that crowded sabbath. Clarke and Wesley explain the timing — “when the sun was set… the Sabbath ended,” reckoned sunset to sunset, and only then was it held lawful to carry the sick (John 5:10); Robertson paints the vivid sunset scene as the day’s news spread. Theophylact marks the people’s zeal, bringing their sick “not deterred by the lateness of the day,” held back only by the sabbath scruple. As the sun goes down the whole town streams to Peter’s door, and the Lord, untiring, lays his hands on “every one of them” and heals them all. The witnesses mark both the eagerness of the people and the Lord’s individual care — not a general word over the crowd but a hand laid on each — the day of teaching and deliverance closing in a flood of mercy poured out on the afflicted of Capernaum.
41:
On Luke 4:41 — “And devils also came out of many, crying out… Thou art Christ the Son of God. And he, rebuking them, suffered them not to speak” — the witnesses mark the demons’ confession and the Lord’s refusal of it.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect “came out” (one after another), and that “Thou art the Son of God” is a more definite confession of deity than the synagogue demon’s, like the Father’s word at the baptism (Luke 3:22). The text, he adds, rightly reads “demons,” not “devils.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem marks the testimony: “this Christ… the Jews denied, but the devils confessed.”

The verse joins the casting out of many demons to the healings, and records again the strange phenomenon of the synagogue: the spirits, expelled, cry out the truth of who Christ is — “Christ the Son of God” — and again the Lord silences them. He “suffered them not to speak,” refusing testimony from such mouths.

So the witnesses dwell on the irony and the wisdom. The demons confess what the Jews deny: the powers of darkness, in their terror, proclaim the deity that unbelief refuses. Yet the Lord will not be witnessed to by devils. The witnesses give the reasons elsewhere — that truth from such a source would taint the testimony, that his hour for open proclamation was not come, that the confession sprang from terror, not faith. Cyril’s contrast stands as the verse’s lesson: it is the shame of the unbelieving that the devils knew and owned the Christ whom they would not receive, and the wisdom of the Lord that he accepted no herald whose witness could only do harm.

So the witnesses dwell on the irony and the wisdom of the scene. Robertson notes the imperfect “came out” (one after another) and that “Thou art the Son of God” is a more definite confession of deity than the synagogue demon’s, echoing the Father’s word at the baptism (Luke 3:22); Cyril of Jerusalem marks the testimony — “this Christ… the Jews denied, but the devils confessed.” Yet the Lord “suffered them not to speak,” refusing testimony from such mouths — for truth from that source would taint it, his hour for open proclamation was not come, and the confession sprang from terror, not faith. The witnesses thus read the standing lesson: it is the shame of unbelief that the devils owned the Christ whom men would not receive, and the wisdom of the Lord that he accepted no herald whose witness could only do harm.
42:
On Luke 4:42 — “And when it was day, he departed and went into a desert place: and the people sought him, and came unto him, and stayed him, that he should not depart” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s retirement and the people’s pursuit.

The Eastern fathers read the departure. Chrysostom: when he had “bestowed sufficient favour upon the people by miracles, it was necessary for Him to depart,” for “miracles are always thought greater when the worker is gone.” A Greek expositor notes he went “into the desert… and prayed; not that he needed prayer, but as an example.” The grammatical-historical Robertson, harmonising Mark 1:35, sets the withdrawal “a great while before day” for prayer after “the excitement of the previous sabbath,” the multitudes then seeking him out. Clarke prefers the stronger reading, the people “earnestly sought him.”

The verse sets two movements side by side: the Lord withdrawing to a solitary place, and the people pressing after to detain him. They had tasted his power and would keep him for themselves; they “stayed him, that he should not depart.” Their eagerness was real, if mixed — partly hunger for his word and works, partly the wish to hold so great a benefactor in their own town.

So the witnesses find in the scene both the Lord’s example and his larger purpose. He sought the desert for prayer, teaching his people, though he needed it not himself, to withdraw to God from the press of even fruitful labour. And he would not be detained by Capernaum’s wish to monopolise him, for, as the next verse declares, he was sent to many cities. The witnesses read here the balance of the ministry: communion with the Father in secret, and a mission too wide to be confined to one grateful town — the Saviour breaking away from the crowd’s embrace to carry the gospel further.

So the witnesses find in the scene both the Lord’s example and his larger purpose. Chrysostom: having “bestowed sufficient favour… by miracles, it was necessary for Him to depart,” and he went “into the desert… and prayed; not that he needed prayer, but as an example.” Robertson, harmonising Mark 1:35, sets the withdrawal before day for prayer after the sabbath’s excitement; the people then “earnestly sought” him (Clarke) and would detain him. The witnesses read the balance of the ministry: communion with the Father in secret, sought even amid fruitful labour, and a mission too wide to be confined to one grateful town. The Lord will not be monopolised by Capernaum’s wish to hold him; breaking from the crowd’s embrace, he withdraws to pray and then presses on — teaching his people both to seek God in solitude and to refuse to let even welcome detain them from the wider work.
43:
On Luke 4:43 — “I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s sense of his wide commission.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the words. Robertson: “I must” (me dei) marks the urge to go “to the other cities also” — to all, “not to a favoured few”; and “for therefore was I sent” (a phrase “of Johannine ring”), Christ being “the great Apostle of God to men.” Ellicott notes the verb is literally “to evangelise,” distinct from the common “preach” — “to declare the glad tidings of the kingdom” — with a special reference to the cities of Galilee. Clarke marks that to “proclaim the kingdom of God was the Messiah’s great work,” the healings being its “emblematical and secondary” proof.

The verse gives the Lord’s own reason for breaking from Capernaum. He was sent not to one city but to many; the preaching of the kingdom, not the working of cures, was his “great work,” and that work could not be confined to a single grateful crowd. The “I must” expresses the inward constraint of a divine commission.

So the witnesses find here the ordering of Christ’s ministry and a pattern for his servants. The miracles, glorious as they were, were the seal and the secondary thing; the proclamation of the kingdom was the end for which he was sent. He would not let even the most willing town detain him from the wider mission, nor let the demand for healing crowd out the preaching of the gospel. The witnesses read in “for therefore am I sent” the heart of his self-understanding: an Apostle of God, constrained to carry the good news of the kingdom to all, refusing to be the private possession of any.

So the witnesses find here the ordering of Christ’s ministry and a pattern for his servants. Robertson reads the inward constraint — “I must” — to evangelise “the other cities also… not… a favoured few,” Christ being “the great Apostle of God to men”; Ellicott notes the verb is “to declare the glad tidings of the kingdom,” distinct from the common “preach”; Clarke, that to proclaim the kingdom was “the Messiah’s great work,” the healings its “secondary” proof. The witnesses thus see the miracles, glorious as they were, as the seal and the secondary thing; the preaching of the kingdom was the end for which he was sent. He would not let even the most willing town detain him from the wider mission, nor let the demand for healing crowd out the gospel — an Apostle of God constrained to carry the good news of the kingdom to all, the private possession of none.
44:
On Luke 4:44 — “And he preached in the synagogues of Galilee” — the witnesses mark the summary of the first Galilean tour.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson reads the periphrastic imperfect “was preaching” as “describing his first tour of Galilee in accord with the purpose just stated,” the details to be filled in from Mark 1:39. Gill notes simply that he preached “in the several synagogues that were in different cities and towns throughout all Galilee.”

The verse is the fulfilment, in act, of the resolve just declared. Having said “I must preach… to other cities,” the Lord at once does so, going from synagogue to synagogue through Galilee. The brief notice gathers up a whole circuit of labour into a single line.

So the witnesses close the chapter on the note with which the ministry rightly proceeds: the steady, itinerant preaching of the kingdom. The wilderness temptation overcome, the Nazareth rejection borne, the Capernaum wonders wrought, the Lord settles to his great work — carrying the gospel through the synagogues of the whole region. The periphrastic tense, the witnesses note, paints not a single sermon but a continuing habit, a tour of grace through Galilee. The chapter that opened with the Spirit leading him into conflict ends with the Spirit-empowered Christ going from town to town, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom of God to all who would hear.
